text: These tubes must withstand neutron doses of the order of $10^{23}$ neutrons/cm² and must have very low permeability to gases (because of ${}^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ entrapment) and fused salts.
The proposed molten-salt breeder reactors<sup>1</sup> will require some advances in materials technology.
From a materials standpoint, the reactor is easily divided into two sections: (1) the core and (2) the reactor vessel and associated piping.

The requirements of the material for use within the core are (1) good moderation, (2) low neutron absorption, (3) compatibility with the molten salt-Hastelloy N system, (4) low permeability to both salt and fission gases, (5) fabricable into tubular shapes, (6) capable of being joined to the rest of the system, and (7) capable of maintaining all the above properties after accumulated neutron doses of $10^{23}$ neutrons/ $\mathsf{cm}^2$ .

The main additional requirements are that we develop the technology for producing tubular shapes of a comparable material with slightly improved gas permeability and then demonstrate that this material will retain its integrity to neutron doses of $10^{23}$ neutrons/ $\mathsf{cm}^2$ .
Available data on the radiation damage of graphite to doses of 2 to $3 \times 10^{22}$ neutrons/ $\mathsf{cm}^2$ indicate that the material is capable of the anticipated doses.

The requirements of a material for this application include (1) resistance to corrosion by fluoride salts, (2) compatibility with the core material, (3) capable of being fabricated into complicated shapes by conventional processes such as rolling, forging, and welding, (4) good mechanical strength and ductility at temperatures over the expected service range of 100 to $1300^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ , and (5) capable of maintaining
We have found that a slightly modified alloy containing 0.5 wt % Ti has good weldability and reasonable resistance to radiation damage.
A molybdenum concentration range of 15 to $20\%$ was selected, since this yielded single-phase alloys with their inherent metallurgical stability.

Creep-Rupture Properties of Hastelloy N at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , Heat 5065.
This supports the hypothesis that the damage is due to the thermal neutrons with the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ transmutation to lithium and helium thought to be the most probable reaction.
This type of irradiation damage has been found to be quite general for iron- and nickel-base structural alloys.[6]
The normal alloys contain 30 to 100 ppm B; and, when exposed to thermal neutrons, the transmutation of the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ just discussed is by far the predominant source of helium.

This source of helium predominates after all the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ has been transmuted (requiring a thermal dose of approximately $10^{21}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ ) or in materials where the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ content is extremely low.
The main source of boron in commercial alloys is the melting crucible, and we have found that with reasonable care commercial heats can be prepared with boron in the range 1-5 ppm.

However, the properties of irradiated metal of this low boron content are no better than those of metal containing 50 ppm B, indicating that the threshold helium levels necessary for damage can be produced in metal containing below 1 ppm B.
Figure 12 shows the simpler microstructure of the alloy containing $12\%$ Mo.
Effect of Molybdenum Content on the Microstructure of Ni-7% Cr-0.2% Mn-0.05% C Alloys After Annealing 1 hr at $2150^{\circ}$ F.
The addition of small amounts (approx 0.5 wt %) of Ti, Zr, and Hf reduces the irradiation damage problem significantly.
Figure 13 illustrates the fact that several alloys have been developed with properties after irradiation that are superior to those of unirradiated standard Hastelloy N.
We are also initiating the procurement of 1500 lb commercial melts of some of the more attractive compositions.
Comparison of the Postirradiation Creep Properties of Several Hastelloy N Alloys at $1202^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Studies on molten fluoride mixtures for reactor applications began in the early 1950's and gave primary consideration to the compatibility of these salt mixtures with structural materials.

In the intervening 15 years, an extensive corrosion program has been conducted at ORNL with several families of fluoride mixtures using both commercial and developmental high-temperature alloys.[7-15] As a consequence, the corrosion
595-604 in Fluid Fuel Reactors, Addison-Wesley, Reading, Pa., 1958.
Further details concerning corrosion mechanisms in fluoride melts have been presented previously.[15]

The original studies that serve as a base for the MSBR corrosion studies were conducted at ORNL as part of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project.[7-13] Work on this project was aimed at a $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ maximum temperature.

Nickel alloys were shown to be the most promising for containing the fluorides; however, strength considerations restricted the candidate materials to the commercial nickel-chromium group, with Inconel 600 receiving the most attention.
The alloy system used as the basis for this development was composed of nickel with a primary strengthening addition of 15 to $20\%$ Mo.
Evaluations of other strengthening additions culminated in the selection of an alloy containing $16\%$ Mo, $7\%$ Cr, and $4\%$ Fe (INOR-8, now Hastelloy N).
In the first phase, the corrosion properties of 13 fluoride salt mixtures were compared in Hastelloy N thermal-convection loops operated for 1000 hr.

Specific salt mixtures, whose compositions are listed in Table 4, were selected to provide an evaluation of (1) the corrosion properties of beryllium-bearing fuels, (2) the corrosion properties of beryllium-fluoride mixtures containing large quantities of thorium, and (3) the corrosion properties of nonfuel-bearing fluoride coolant salts.

These loops operated with maximum fuel-salt temperatures of 1250 to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , maximum coolant-salt temperatures of 1050 to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and all with approximately $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ temperature drops.
A total of 49 thermal loops and 15 forced-circulation loops were operated during these phases of the program.
The maximum attack found after 8760 hr in the phase-II tests was a limited surface roughening and pitting to a depth of $1/2$ mil.

The temperature dependence of the maximum corrosion rate, as judged from total weight losses of specimens, was less than a factor of 5 over the temperature range investigated (Table 5).

The full-power operation of the reactor began in March 1965.

The first group of metal and graphite surveillance specimens was removed in July 1966 after exposure to the reactor core environment for 7612 Mwhr which included a thermal exposure of $4800\mathrm{hr}$ at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The peak thermal dose was $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ and the peak fast dose $>1.2$ Mev was $3 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ .
The concentration of chromium in the fuel salt increased from an initial value of 37 to 48 ppm where it remained fairly steady during the 7612 Mwhr run.
The iron and nickel concentrations remained constant at values of 10 and 1 ppm, respectively.

Several of the graphite and metal specimens were given to the Reactor Chemistry Division for analysis with respect to fission product content.[17] The graphite specimens have been stored and mechanical property tests will be run in the near future.
17 and was present on both the surveillance and the control specimens.
Electron microprobe examination showed that the carbon content of the edge of the specimen ranged from 0.3 to 1.2 wt % compared with a value of 0.05% for the interior of the sample.
Edge of Hastelloy N Specimen Exposed to Molten Salt for 4800 hr at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
metal surfaces that were separated from the graphite 1/16 in.
The total elongation at fracture when deformed at a strain rate of $0.05\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ is shown as a function of temperature in Fig.
Both heats show some reduction in ductility at low temperatures in the irradiated condition with a greater reduction being observed for heat 5085.

At temperatures above $932^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the ductility of the irradiated and the control material decreased with increasing temperature, with the irradiated material showing a greater loss in ductility.
At temperatures above 1202 to $1292^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the control material exhibited improved ductility, whereas the ductility of the irradiated material continued to decrease.
Comparative Tensile Ductilities of MSRE Surveillance Specimens and Their Controls at a Strain Rate of $0.05\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ .

Heat 5081 had been irradiated previously in the ORR.[18] The ORR experiment was run at $1292^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to a thermal dose of $9 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ and the material was in the as-received condition.
The MSRE surveillance specimens were run at $1202^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to a thermal dose of $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ and the preirradiation anneal was different.
Figure 19 shows that the postirradiation ductilities of heat 5081 after both experiments are very similar.
The surveillance specimens were exposed to a thermal dose of $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm².

(The MSRE vessel will reach this dose after about 150,000 Mwhr of operation.) This burned out about $30\%$ of the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ and produced a helium content of about $10^{-5}$ atom fraction in both heats.
Our work indicates a saturation in the degree of radiation damage at a helium atom fraction of about $10^{-5}$ and we feel that the properties of the material will not deteriorate further.
The MSRE has since operated for a total of approximately 32,500 Mwhr.
The chromium content of the salt has been somewhat higher during this run at approximately 60 ppm.
We removed a group of surveillance specimens (graphite and Hastelloy N) for study about May 15, 1967.
The oxidation rate of the binary nickel-molybdenum alloy passes through a maximum for the alloy containing $15\%$ Mo, and the scale formed by the oxidation is $\mathrm{NiMoO_4}$ and NiO.
from above $1400^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ to below $660^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ , the $\mathrm{NiMoO_4}$ undergoes a phase transformation that causes the protective scale on the oxidized metal to spall.
Similarly, the oxidation rate of nickel-molybdenum alloys containing chromium passes through a maximum as the chromium content of the alloys is increased from 2 to $6\%$ Cr.
Alloys containing more than $6\%$ Cr are insensitive to thermal cycling and to the molybdenum content because the oxide scale is predominantly stable $\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}$ .
An abrupt decrease (by a factor of about 40) in the oxidation rate at $1800^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ is observed when the chromium content is increased from 5.9 to $6.2\%$ .
The oxidation resistance of Hastelloy N is excellent, and continuous operation at temperatures up to $1800^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ is feasible.
Intermittent use at temperatures as high as $1900^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ could be tolerated.

For temperatures up to $1200^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ , the oxidation rate is not measurable; it is essentially zero after 1000 hr of exposure in static air, as well as in nitrogen containing small quantities of air (the MSRE cell environment).
It is estimated that oxidation of 0.001 to 0.002 in. would occur in 100,000 hr of operation at $1200^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .
Hastelloy N does not react noticeably with nitrogen at temperatures up to $2200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the absence of irradiation.
In parts of the system Hastelloy N will be exposed to supercritical steam having a maximum temperature of $1050^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ and a pressure of 3800 psi.

The stress-corrosion type of failure in boiling water and in superheated steam systems has been observed for the austenitic stainless steels in several nuclear systems.[19,20] The resistance of high-nickel alloys to this type of attack has also been established and it seems reasonable that our system should utilize such an alloy.[19,20]
Hastelloy N has been included in steam-metal compatibility programs at BNWL, $^{21,22}$ and some of the results of these studies are summarized in Table 8.
It also compared very favorably with the other nickel-base alloys that were included in these evaluation programs (e.g., Hastelloy X, Inconel 600, and Incoloy 800).
Although the depth of attack varied slightly with steam pressure and oxygen content, a rather pessimistic value for the corrosion rate is about 0.1 mil attack in 2400 hr.
Assuming a linear rate of attack, which again is on the pessimistic side, the depth of attack in 20 years would be about 7 mils.
Assuming the more realistic parabolic behavior, the expected depth of attack would be only 0.8 mil.

Individual melts up to 10,000 lb and a total quantity of almost 200,000 lb were produced.
The standard finished annealing temperature for this alloy is $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After final fabrication, all material was stress relieved at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
For any heat treatment the cooling rate to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was limited to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ per hour per inch of thickness.
The slow cooling helped to impart dimensional stability and improved the ductility in the 1400 to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ range.

section: Welding and Brazing of Hastelloy N
text: Cracking was shown to be associated with the segregation of alloying elements in the heat-affected zone of Hastelloy N weldments.[23] Figure 22 shows the microstructural changes that can occur as a result of welding; microprobe analyses of these samples showed that the brittle eutectic-type structure had a markedly different composition from that of the matrix.

The room- and elevated-temperature shear strengths of Hastelloy N joints brazed with Au-18% Ni filler metal have also been determined.[26] Joints tested at room temperature had an average shear strength of 73,000 psi, while those tested at $1300^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ possessed an average strength of 18,000 psi.
exchanger for the MSRE.[28] Figure 23 shows the completed tube bundle in its handling fixture.
Figure 24 is a sketch of the joint.
Welding procedures have been developed for joining Hastelloy N to several different materials, including Inconel 600, austenitic stainless steels, and nickel.

While many remote joining methods have been developed for the nuclear industry, they were always for special applications such as replacing the seal weld on a vessel, or making a closure weld for a capsule or a fuel element.[29] Commercial equipment is not generally available for such applications.

The nearest approaches to developed remote maintenance systems for a large reactor were one designed and partially developed by Westinghouse Electric Corporation, Atomic Power Department, for use with the proposed Pennsylvania Advanced Reactor,[30] and one at Atomics International for use with a large sodium graphite reactor.[31] However, these projects were terminated before the developed techniques could be demonstrated.

A value for the permeability to gases has not been specified yet but a very low one is desirable to minimize the rate of diffusion of xenon into the graphite and thereby minimize the loss of neutrons to $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ .

Finally the graphite in the MSBR will be irradiated to a dose as high as $2 \times 10^{22}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ ( $E > 0.18$ MeV) per year and the tubes must perform satisfactorily at this irradiation level for at least three and possibly five years to have a practical power reactor.
It is basically a petroleum needle coke that is bonded with coal-tar pitch, extruded to rough shape, and heated to $5072^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
All components are fired to $5072^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ or higher.
Its properties are tabulated in Table 9.

In manufacturing the material for the MSRE, experimental equipment and processes were used on a commercial scale for the first time by the Carbon Products Division of Union Carbide Corporation to produce bars 2.50 in. square and 72 in. long which were machined to the shapes required for the MSRE.

small; over $95\%$ of the entrances are $< 0.4 \mu$ in diameter. $^{36}$ The molten salts do not wet the graphite, and calculations indicate that pressures in excess of 300 psig would be required to force salt into these accessible voids.
However, 0.50-in.-thick sections (heaviest wall for MSBR fuel tubes) have been made successfully without cracks.
Some of the more stringent specifications on graphite for MSBR will be on permeability.40 Values will be specified for permeability to both molten salts and to helium.
The screening test for salt permeability is to expose evacuated specimens for 100 hr to molten salt at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ under a pressure of 150 psig.
The MSRE design specifications required that less than $0.5\%$ of the bulk volume of the graphite should be filled with salt and that the salt should not penetrate the graphite matrix.

Specimens of grade CGB had an average of less than $0.2\%$ of their bulk volume permeated by salt in these screening tests, and the salt distribution was limited to shallow penetrations at the surfaces and along cracks which intersected the exterior surfaces.
27, did not permeate the matrix.
We would like a graphite for the MSBR with a helium permeability of $10^{-7}\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ .
While it has been possible to obtain graphites meeting the salt-permeability requirements, it has not been possible to reduce the gas permeability to the $10^{-7}$ level.
Crack-free CGB graphite in the MSRE exhibited average helium permeability values of $3 \times 10^{-4}\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ .
At present, it would appear that values of $10^{-5}\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ would be about all that can be produced by present technology.
Experimental and other special graphite pipes have been produced with permeabilities less than $10^{-6}\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$
Such a seal may be a penetrating, integral skin of graphite or a thin coating of $^{92}\mathrm{Mo}$ or Nb.
The surface coating would be quite thin (approx 0.0001 in.) but would be applied under conditions where the metal would be linked into the pore structure so that good adherence would be ensured.
The tensile and flexural strength measurements on grade CGB graphite for the MSRE averaged 1900 and 4300 psi, respectively, despite the presence of cracks in the graphite.
There were no values less than the minima of 1500 and 3000 psi specified for the tensile and flexural strengths, respectively.
However, under fast-neutron irradiation, the differences in shrinkage rates across the 1/2-in. wall of a pipe caused by flux gradients can produce high stresses.

Specimens that were not appreciably affected by cracks (the open circles) yielded an average strength of 5440 psi and a modulus of elasticity of $3.2 \times 10^{6}$ psi, corresponding to an unusually strong graphite.

Pipes of grade H-315-A (Table 10) have been made that are approximately 4.5 in. in diameter with 0.50-in.-wall thickness which would correspond to the large MSBR pipes.
These pipes had an average bulk density of $1.83\mathrm{g/cm}^3$ and radially they had a permeability to helium of $2 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-2}\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ .

Pore spectrum measurements indicated that approximately $50\%$ of the pore volume had pore entrance diameters between 0.5 and $3.5\mu$ and less than $15\%$ of the pore volume had pore entrance diameters $>3.5\mu$ .

Of course, in fabricating graphite for MSBR use one would want to optimize the base stock for impregnation which would mean that the base stock would be fabricated with pore entrances less than $1\mu$ in diameter.

With all the tons of graphite that have been used in nuclear reactors, most of the work on graphite-to-metal joining seems to have been done in small programs at ORNL $^{45,46}$ and Atomics International.
The ductile and corrosion-resistant alloy, $82\%$ Au-18% Ni, was chosen as a starting point for molten-salt applications.

As a result, alloys in both the Au-Ni-Mo and Au-Ni-Ta systems were developed<sup>45</sup> which fulfilled the above requirements with the possible exception of nuclear stability in a high neutron flux (gold will transmute to mercury in high flux fields).
The most promising alloy contains 35 Au-35 Ni-30 Mo (wt %) and has been designated as ANM-16.
Graphite-to-graphite and graphite-to-molybdenum joints have been made with ANM-16 by induction brazing in an inert atmosphere and vacuum brazing in a muffle furnace.
A brazing temperature of 2282 to $2372^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was used.
Several components have been brazed with this alloy, the largest of which have been 1.50-in.
OD × 0.31-in.-wall graphite pipes with molybdenum end caps and sleeves.
A short section of graphite pipe with a brazed graphite-to-metal end closure successfully contained molten salts for 500 hr at $1292^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and a pressure of 150 psig.
The graphite pipe was machined to 1.25-in.
Molybdenum end caps were brazed to the ends of the pipe using braze metal ANM-16.
No leak occurred during the 500-hr test period, and posttest radiographic examination indicated that the salt was contained as planned.
This alloy has a composition of $35\%$ Ni- $60\%$ Pd- $5\%$ Cr and has exhibited brazing characteristics very much like the $35\%$ Au- $35\%$ Ni- $30\%$ Mo alloy.
The brazing alloy wet both materials and appeared to form a joint as good as that obtained with ANM-16.
Joints of graphite to molybdenum have been brazed with this alloy and survived a $1300^{\circ}$ F corrosion test (LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ ) of 10,000 hr with no attack.
A 20,000-hr corrosion test has accumulated 10,700 hr to date.
Chromium, the carbide former, may be tied up in the form of a corrosion-resistant carbide, $\mathrm{Cr}_3\mathrm{C}_2$ , to such an extent that the chromium will not cause a corrosion problem.[47]
Techniques are commercially available for joining graphite to itself.[48] Most techniques are based on the use of furfural alcohol and graphite powder.
The mechanism of sealing is by polymerization of the alcohol at temperatures to $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The polymerization would be followed by a graphitizing treatment at about $4892^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

section: Compatibility of Graphite with Molten Salts
text: The tendency for Hastelloy N to be carburized was investigated in static pots containing LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ (67-32-1 mole %) and a graphite at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for times ranging from 2000 to 12,000 hr.

section: Compatibility of Graphite with Molten Salts
text: No carburization was metallographically detected on specimens from any of the above tests.[49] Tensile properties were determined on specimens included in each test.

section: Compatibility of Graphite with Molten Salts
text: For the purpose of evaluating the compatibility of graphite and Hastelloy N in a circulating fluoride fuel, a Hastelloy N forced-convection loop was operated for 8850 hr.

section: Compatibility of Graphite with Molten Salts
text: The loop operated at a maximum temperature of $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and circulated a fluoride mixture of $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - UF_4}$ .

section: Compatibility of Graphite with Molten Salts
text: The tests indicated that (1) there was no corrosion or erosion of the graphite by the flowing salt; (2) there was very little permeation of the graphite by the salt and the permeation that occurred was uniform throughout the graphite rods; (3) the various Hastelloy N loop components exposed to the salt were not carburized; (4) the Hastelloy N components exposed to the salt and graphite were negligibly attacked; and (5) with the possible exception of oxygen contamination, the salt appeared to have undergone no chemical changes as a result of exposure to the graphite test specimens.

section: Compatibility of Graphite with Molten Salts
text: In-reactor tests $^{50}$ confirmed this good compatibility.
Although graphite has been used successfully in reactors for over 20 years, it is only within the past 10 years that the effects of irradiation have been studied intensively.

These systems will, however, subject the graphite to much higher neutron doses [for a 10-year life, $2 \times 10^{23}$ neutrons/cm² ( $>0.18$ MeV)] than have been or are being obtained in any other reactor system.
Although there is no experimental evidence beyond $2 \times 10^{22}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ , several factors lead to optimism about the ability of graphite to sustain much greater damage.

The first factor is the recognition that in the $1292^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ temperature range the contractual dimensional changes for the first $10^{22}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ are due to the repair of the damage caused by cooling the graphite from its graphitization temperature.
Doses above $10^{22}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ produce an expansion in the "c" direction and a contraction in the "a" direction.
Even so, measurements obtained from irradiated pyrolytic graphites indicate that a deformation rate of about $1\%$ shear strain per $10^{21}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ must be absorbed.
Thus, for a 5-year life, the graphite tubes operating in a flux region of $6 \times 10^{14}$ neutrons $\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ sec $^{-1}$ must absorb an internal deformation of about $100\%$ shear strain.
Very fine crystallite pyrocarbons have been irradiated under conditions producing crystallite shearing rates of $60\%$ per $10^{22}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ (ref.
51) and have demonstrated the ability to absorb $160\%$ shear strain without observable internal cracking or loss of integrity.

Using a conservative estimate of $2.4 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^2/$ neutron as the growth rate and a $10\%$ flux drop across the wall, a differential growth rate of $2.4 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{~cm}^2/$ neutron is obtained.
Thus, a strain rate of $1.2 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{~cm}^2/$ neutron is obtained for comparison to an estimated creep rate coefficient of $4 \times 10^{-27} (\mathrm{psi})^{-1}$ .
\sigma = \frac {\dot {\varepsilon}}{K} = \frac {1 .

For lifetimes of $1 \times 10^{23}$ , $2 \times 10^{23}$ , and $4 \times 10^{23}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ (corresponding to 5-, 10-, and 20-year lifetimes), the strain to be absorbed would be 1.2, 2.4, and $4.8\%$ , respectively.
It has been demonstrated that graphite can absorb strains in excess of $2\%$ in $10^{22}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ under stresses in excess of 3000 psi without fracture.
There is also some evidence that growth rates of isotropic graphites will diminish after a dose of $10^{22}$ neutron/cm $^2$ .
Although there is no direct evidence that graphite can sustain $1.2\%$ strain in 5 years of very high dose and low stress, the indirect evidence does indicate that a failure is improbable.
During this period, most testing will be on laboratory-size vacuum-melts and on small (100 lb) commercial melts.
Large commercial heats (1500 lb) of a few compositions will be tested during the second and third years.
When the composition is firmly established, we shall procure a few full-scale commercial heats (10,000 lb).
The fabrication problems fall into two categories: (1) the original construction and (2) maintenance of the system.
This combining of problems will greatly reduce the effort required for the first 2 years.
5. development of remote welding techniques for a variety of Hastelloy N joints from 4 in. to 6 ft in diameter,
Heats showing tendencies to crack will be studied on the "Gleeble"61 which will be programmed to magnify any effects resulting from heating.
Procedures for making tube-to-header joints for small tubes (0.50 in.) were developed and used successfully for the MSRE heat exchanger.

These phases of the program will be part of the maintenance equipment development.[62] Several different types of remote welds will be required and they will be sufficiently different to require separate development.
The major problem with the Hastelloy N-to-Hastelloy N butt joints in tubes will be the limited space around each pipe; this is approximately 1 in.
This technique, which was developed for the MSRE, makes use of a conical-type joint in which one member must either twist or slide as the braze metal melts.[63]
A single development effort should suffice for all the large (approx 12-in.-diam) remote pipe joints.
Welds similar to these were developed by Westinghouse for the Pennsylvania Advanced Reactor,[64] and their procedures will serve as a starting point.
The positioning equipment for such a job would be similar to that used to cut small pieces from the HRT core tank.[65]

evaluated for this material are (1) determining its behavior at radiation dose levels as high as possible, (2) joining of graphite to itself and to structural materials, (3) fabrication development to yield a low gas permeability, (4) evaluation and characterization of the modified or improved graphite, (5) determining its compatibility when used with Hastelloy N in a system circulating fused salts at elevated temperatures, and (6) fabrication of the graphite into engineering systems and evaluating its performance.
In about 1 year, prepare a specification for a pilot-size batch of the most promising type, or types, of graphite.

To demonstrate the ability of graphite to successfully retain its integrity after exposure to dose levels of $1 \times 10^{23}$ neutrons/cm² (5-year life), we plan to irradiate graphite to as near this dose as possible.
This will require the irradiations to be performed in reactors that have fast fluxes of $10^{15}$ neutrons cm⁻² sec⁻¹ or greater to obtain the data in a reasonable time.
Approximate values of the time required to reach a dose of $1 \times 10^{23}$ neutrons/cm², cost per year, and facility size are listed in Table 11.

It is, therefore, proposed to use HFIR to demonstrate the ability of the graphite to retain its integrity after an exposure of $1 \times 10^{23}$ neutrons/cm².
Braze joints of several designs will be evaluated using the $35\%$ Ni- $60\%$ Pd- $5\%$ Cr alloy and pure copper as brazing materials.
These joints will be made using the 5-in.-OD $\times 1/2$ -in.-wall graphite pipes that are presently in the MSBR design.
As was pointed out in the previous discussion, obtaining graphite with the very low permeability of $1 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ will be very difficult.
